1: <header>Cradle to farm gate life cycle assessment of oilseed crops production in Iran </header>
Majid Dekamina, Morteza Barmaki, Amin Kanoonib, Seyed Reza Mosavi Meshkini

a Department of Agronomy & Plant Breeding, Faculty of Agriculture Sciences & Natural Resource, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Daneshgah Street, 56199-11367, Ardabil, Iran b Faculty of Department of Water Engineering, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, Daneshgah Street, 56199-11367, Ardabil, Iran c Faculty of Department of Environment, 1983963113, Zanjan, Iran

ARTICLE INFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Life cycle assessment Environmental impacts Oilseed crops Agriculture Optimization Iran Land use|The goal of this research was to evaluate the environmental effects of the production of soybean (Glycine max), sunflower (Helianthus annuus L.), and canola (Brassica napus) oilseed crops in Ardabil in Iran. Lifecycle assess- ment was conducted to compare environmental burdens, water demand, and land use of these three crops. Four categories of environmental impacts were used to define in this method. The categories are eutrophication, acidification, global warming potential, and photochemical oxidation. Additional categories were water de- pendence and land use. These environmental effects followed the same pattern for the three oilseed crops. The results showed that sunflower demonstrated a higher environmental load in five of the six categories because of its lower seed yield and higher water and energy demands. The results indicate that production of 1 T of soybean and canola requires 370 and 471 m² of land, respectively. Production of same quantity of sunflower required 426 m² of land. Sunflower required 1.59 and 1.25 times more water than the canola and soybean respectively, to produce the same quantity of oilseed. It was found that the effects from the three crops comes generally from manufactured fertilizer, manure, diesel combustion, agricultural practices, and electricity for irrigation. It can be said that the indirect effects associated with these inputs are related to producing and processing, which had higher impacts than those of the direct effects.
2: The controversy about sustainable agriculture has been extended to consider the impact of agricultural production. Population growth has escalated the need for food, water, and renewable energy resources, which require development of new knowledge about and technology for production systems using sustainable development frameworks (Chen and Zhang, 2015; Ruviaro et al., 2012).

As a consequence of agricultural reliance on natural resources and processes, a complicated relationship exists between agriculture and the environment. Agricultural activities can be both advantageous and disadvantageous for the environment. The disadvantages include overuse of farm input such as pesticides, fertilizers, and non-renewable energies, in addition to land occupation, destruction of wildlife habitat, and decreased biodiversity. Srivastava et al. (2016) reported that minimizing the negative effects and maximizing the positive effects provides a challenge to assure its sustainability. Appropriate policies are required to determine guidelines and understand how the use of sources of energy and materials effect food production.
Crop production and processing use different systems to determine

water use, energy efficiency, and environmental emissions between sectors (Cellura et al., 2012; Chen and Zhang, 2015). The complicated conditions that result from production of different agricultural products in developing countries, such as Iran, that should be considered more closely (Dekamin et al., 2015; Khoshnevisan et al., 2014; Rajaeifar et al., 2014). Agricultural activities have grown quickly over recent decades in Iran; their environmental impacts have also escalated (Fao, 2007). Major environmental issues in Iran include water shortages, nitrate leaching into ground water (Jalali, 2011; Mahvi et al., 2005), soil erosion, and the release of pesticides and P-based fertilizers into water resources (Fadaei et al., 2012; Kafilzadeh et al., 2012). Because a legislative gap exists about environmental impact assessment of agri- cultural activities in Iran; thus, little attention has been paid to reducing environmental emissions and use of resources of the agricultural pro- ducts (Dekamin et al., 2015; Khoshnevisan et al., 2015). Standard procedures should be used to assess agricultural activity to introduce environmentally-friendly practices and products. Crops production is a system having high environmental impacts. Different tools are available for assessment of the environmental impact of agricultural products. The most comprehensive of these is life cycle assessment (LCA) (Goglio

et al., 2015; Navarro et al., 2016). As a scientific methodology, LCA is capable of examining and comparing resource use and emissions asso- ciated with agricultural production systems (Dekamin et al., 2015; Khoshnevisan et al., 2014; Rajaeifar et al., 2014; Romero-Gámez et al., 2014).

Environmental assessment of agricultural products do not consider the complete lifecycle of a product (O'Brien et al., 2012). The most common environmental impact categories identified by LCA of crop production systems include acidification, eutrophication, global warming, land use, depletion of resources (e.g., non-renewable energy and water during crop production) (Abeliotis et al., 2016; Christoforou et al., 2016; Foteinis and Chatzisymeon, 2016; Van Stappen et al., 2016). The present study used LCA to evaluate the materials used, energy consumption, and related environmental emissions from cradle to farm gate for soybean (Glycine max), sunflower (Helianthus annuus L.), and canola (Brassica napus) crops in Ardabil province of Iran.

5: Ardabil province offers highly suitable climatic conditions for growing a variety of agricultural products, including oilseed crops (Asgharipour et al., 2012) (Fig. 1). Soybean is an oil crop used to produce oil globally and in Iran. Recent statistics from the Agricultural Ministry show that the area allotted to soybean in Iran exceeds 84084 ha (Statistics, 2011). Soybean is cultivated in northern Iran, primarily in Ardabil province, where currently more than 20% of the national production is grown. Ardabil ranks second in soybean culti- vation in Iran (Statistics, 2011).

Canola cultivation occupies a total area of 85933 ha in Iran, which resulted in 1640187 of canola in 2011. Ardabil province also ranks second in canola production in the country. Canola is a popular oilseed crop in Iran because of its good ability to adapt to climate change, suitability for mechanization, and low labor requirements.

Sunflower is the next most suitable alternative after canola; it pro- duces a higher rate of oilseed production per ha than canola. Soybean, sunflower, and canola are grown in northwestern Ardabil using crop rotation under conventional tillage. In the conventional oilseed pro- duction herbicide is used for weed growth control. Insecticides, fungi- cides and herbicides, are distributed by tractor spray pump. In Ardabil

The basic guidelines for administering an LCA delineated in ISO standards 14040 and 14044 were observed in this research. LCA is an internationally standardized holistic assessment framework and is widely used to identify potential environmental emissions and re- sources throughout a product's lifecycle, particularly in the agro-food sector (ISO, 2006). Literature on LCA has demonstrated potential ap- plications of LCA in agriculture (Abeliotis et al., 2016; Bernesson et al., 2004; Cellura et al., 2012; Khoshnevisan et al., 2014). The ISO 14040 series indicates that LCA comprises four distinct phases: goal and scope definition; inventory analysis; impact assessment; and interpretation of results (ISO, 2006). Each phase is explained in the following sections. All environmental impacts were analyzed using Simapro V 8.0.1 soft- ware (PRe, 2015).

8: Goals and scope definition are the most important phase of LCA in which the initial decisions are made. These decisions include the ob- jective settings, functional units, system boundaries, and systems to be compared (Guinée, 2002). The goal of the current analysis was to ex- amine the environmental performance of conventional cropping prac- tices of oilseed crops at the local level in Iran (Ardabil province). The study also selected and optimized cultivation, inputs, and equipment which impacts on the environment and minimizes use of land and water resources.

The scope of the present research was limited to oilseed production system using all inputs and outputs of agricultural materials, energy, and water from cradle to farm gate in Ardabil. All oilseed crops need the different agricultural operation and requirements. For example canola, with very small seeds, needs very much care in seed bed pre- paration with at least two pass of roller packer before and after sowing.
9: A functional unit (FU) is a product system performance quantifier and functions as a reference unit in an LCA. FU is a key concept in LCA that facilitates comparison between products (ISO, 2006). All materials, emissions, and water and energy consumption measured per of a crop at 13% humidity are denoted as a FU. The selection of a mass- based FU has the goal of comparing three oilseeds crops Functional units at LCAs for agricultural production systems were used to apply unit area or unit yield, and unit value of production, from a stand point of research objectives. In this paper, FU was set as 1 T of yields, because land use is not directly a service and does not provide a productive function. Moreover, the comparison of unit value of oil seeds is outside the goal of the study (Longo et al., 2017).
10: A system boundary covers the entire production system for soybean, sunflower, and canola. The system boundary involves foreground and background processes. The background processes were raw material extraction, fertilizer, electricity and fuel production, and their trans- portation to the farm gate. The ecoinvent v.3.1 database has been used, because related data were not available in Iran. Foreground processes were field-work processes and emissions to soil, water, and air resulting from agricultural activities. The primary agricultural activities en- compass several steps: soil preparation, sowing, weed control, fertilizer application (e.i. animal manure and mineral fertilizers), and harvesting. The straw was plowed back into the field. As an agricultural manage- ment activity, this process is beneficial because it returns and recycles nutrients, increases oil organic material content, and prevents soil erosion. The fate of the products was not considered (Fig. 2).
Fig. 2. System boundaries and relevant inputs of the soybean, sunflower and canola production systems.

2.2.4. Inventory data collection The agricultural data was obtained from a survey conducted during the 2013-2014 agricultural season. The inventory data was collected based on the commercial production of each crop. The goal of inventory questionnaire was to evaluate type of machinery, inputs and outputs and irrigation. Also, data collection involved face-to-face interview with farmers. The primary and final inventory data was obtained in two stages. The primary stage involved a survey of 45 fields based on field size. The fields had varying social, technical, and economical features. The next stage was to conduct a deep survey on six fields to complete the inventories and validate the primary data, particularly about ma- chinery, irrigation systems, fuel consumption, and other agricultural inputs and outputs. The concentrations of active ingredients in liter of herbicides and pesticides were considered. The medium values for in- puts and outputs were considered by arithmetical average of the farmer's answers. Data associated to the foreground system were largely gathered in field and by questionnaire. Agricultural experts from the Agriculture Faculty of the University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, as well as technical literature validated all the data. All oil crops use the same sources of energy, agricultural inputs, and modes of management. Maintenance of agricultural machinery and Infrastructure were not considered.

15: Published literature on LCA has demonstrated the importance of air emission computations for ammonia (NH3), NO, and N20, which are produced by the addition of synthetic fertilizers during crop produc- tion. Direct emissions from fertilizers were calculated using different procedures. Nitrogen emissions were estimated using guidelines for nitrate (NO3) ammonia (NH3) and nitrous oxide (N20) emissions de- veloped by Brentrup et al. (2001). Phosphate (PO4 emissions were calculated based on Nemecek et al. (2007). Fuel consumption by ma- chinery releases harmful combinations into the air. Emission factors for diesel fuel has been presented by Sahle and Potting (2013). Pesticide, fungicide and herbicide emissions were estimated using guidelines de- veloped by Dijkman et al. (2012).
16: The CML method developed by the Environmental Sciences Institute of Leiden University was adopted in the present study. The rationale for selecting this method is its potential for generating international con- sensus as well as its objectivity (Guinée, 2002). The impact categories chosen for LCA represent the environmental issues associated with the crops produced, study objectives, and scope (ISO, 2006). The lifecycle impacts related to the crops were conducted and the data was quanti- fied using CML 2 Baseline 2000. The impact categories investigated in this research were eutrophication, acidification, global warming po- tential, and photochemical oxidation.
In addition, the impacts of water dependence and land use were examined. These impact categories refer to total water input and land use associated with each crop during the oil crop's lifecycle.

Acidification (AC) is predominantly caused by emissions of H2S, NH3, SOx, and NOx with H2S and NH3. In this impact category, background processes are the major contributors, predominantly the irrigation and utilization of agricultural machinery (combustion diffusion from fuel). More NO and SO2 is released into the atmosphere. The environmental impact of AC derives from the utili- zation of manufactured fertilizers that produce nitrogen-based emis- sions. The production of manure is another hot spot in relation to AC, because of the acidifying emissions associated with manure production.

30: Table 3 allows comparative LCA of the sunflower, soybean, and canola crops per environmental impact category. Soybean seeds showed the best environmental profile regardless of impact category. Sunflower showed the worst environmental results in almost all cate- gories. The environmental results were similar for all three crops, but the sunflower demonstrated the highest environmental load in five out six impact categories. The better results for soybean resulted from the higher seed yield produced in comparison with other oilseed crops and low requirements for agricultural input. The lowest fertilizer require- ments and demand for mineral fertilizers by soybean (P and K-based fertilizers) and the higher oilseed yield produced the lowest impact in all categories.
The environmental burdens were more remarkable for sunflower and canola in comparison with soybean (Table 3). These results stemmed from the lower demand for irrigation water and agricultural

input and lower usage of N, P, and K-based fertilizers (although these increased oilseed yield) in soybean. Canola showed better environ- mental performance than sunflower. Because all three oilseed crops had the same agricultural input requirements and farming operations, the differences in environmental impact were caused by the oilseed yield and the difference in demand for water (Table 2).

The environmental impact was affected by the usage of manu- factured fertilizer and manure, diesel combustion, agricultural prac- tices, and electricity for irrigation. Electricity production, manure, and mineral fertilizers were the hot spots with the highest environmental emissions. There were indirect effects associated with these inputs for production and processing that had a greater impact than those of the direct effects. The amounts and type of fertilizer and electricity were strongly associated with the impact of oilseed production for sunflower. Note that natural gas is the main source of electricity in Iran (Dekamin et al., 2015; Mohammadi et al., 2013). In Ardabil, most farmers use traditional methods (e.g., flood irrigation) that require more water than other irrigation methods. The environmental results indicate a con- version underway in production practices from sunflower and canola to soybean cultivation that considerably reduces agricultural inputs and energy use (electricity and diesel) per ton of oilseed produced.

33: Effective technical and agronomic measures for decreasing water dependence, land use, and environmental impact can be conducted on oilseed production systems. The amounts and types of manufactured fertilizer and manure are strongly associated with emissions from oil- seed production. A higher harvest index can result in major optimiza- tion of the environmental burdens of the production of oilseed crops. Reduction and optimization of the use of manufactured fertilizer should be considered and organic fertilizer should be used rather than manu- factured fertilizers.

One way to increase water use efficiency and decrease diesel con- sumption of the oilseed crops, especially sunflower production, is to adopt minimum tillage or no-tillage for soil surface protection to save water (which increases both diesel use efficiency and tractors use in the field). Because the main land use is constant per unit of ground area, a simple way to decrease land use is to increase crop commercial yield (Iriarte et al., 2010; Romero-Gámez et al., 2014).
The government has subsidized N and P-based fertilizers for the past

four decades. There are no trustworthy annual records on the quantity of fertilizer used by oilseed crops in Ardabil province. Crop growers use higher levels of N and P fertilizers than is recommended by the Soil and Water Research Institute (SWRI) based on soil tests. The amounts of these fertilizers were higher than oilseed crop uptake requirements. The low prices lead to increased application of different types of fertilizer. Higher prices for fertilizer will likely decrease the amount of fertilizer consumption. This policy can cause a sharp decrease in environmental emissions and promote balanced fertilizer application. Studies have shown that balanced fertilizer application ratios could increase the yield levels by up to 60% (Balali et al.; Malakouti et al., 2004). To decrease the environmental impact and higher yield, soil testing on a regional level is necessary.

In Ardabil province, the gap between potential and actual crop yield is particularly large. Enhancing crop yield is expected to have a major decrease in the environmental burden and water use. The environ- mental impact of oilseed production can be alleviated through selection of crops with a better harvest index or by improving nutrient use effi- ciency of the oilseed crops. Improving the harvest index would decrease agricultural inputs, land and water use of oilseed crops per FU. Nevertheless, these improvements will not upgrade the impact of agricultural machinery.

36: A comparative LCA was conducted for three oilseed crops in Ardabil province in Iran. The study drew up an inventory for the production of oilseed crops. The impact categories were EP, AP, GWP, and PO. Additional impact categories were water dependence and land use. The environmental results followed the same pattern for all three crops. Soybean were found to be the best crop in all impact categories. It appears that field emissions derived from application of agricultural inputs are the most important environmental hotspots for all cropping systems.

The results of present study indicate that improvements can de- crease the environmental burden of oilseed production and thereby maximize the water efficiency and land use. Although increasing the yield showed the most remarkable decrease in environmental impacts in all categories, increased energy and water use efficiency, decreased and optimized manufactured fertilizer, and reduced agricultural op- erations also reduced the environmental impacts.

The present study cannot provide a comprehensive view of the en- vironmental burdens for the three oilseed crops in Iran. The primary goal was to specify the best and the most environmentally-friendly crop of the three oilseed crops in terms of their environmental impacts. Assessment of additional impact categories is necessary.

Policy-makers in Iran are becoming increasingly aware of the need to handle crop-related water overuse and environmental emissions. The present study demonstrates the value of LCA in understanding en- vironmental emissions related to oilseed crops and provides valuable information to allow decision-making for environmentally-friendly agriculture practices. These results should be considered to make reg- ulatory goals to increase environmentally-friendly oilseed production in Ardabil province. Regional conditions in oilseed crops were taken into account by developing a regionalized inventory database and by char- acterizing the factors modified to conditions in Ardabil.
